sentences.text: Yes, this is my daughter, Sandra.
She's married to Arnold Wagner, a lovely husband she has.
She has four children-- two girl-- three girls and one boy.
The oldest one is Rachel, Avital, Micky, and the young boy is David.

There-- there is an enormous amount one could say that covers all the aspects of having been brought up with a survivor and the traumas and the nightmares and the getting-- the times that were easier, and the slowly, slowly, the way stories came out, bit by bit-- the inability to talk and then the release.
And it's very interesting that mummy felt she could talk to my husband and to my sister's husband more easily than she ever felt she could talk to my sister and I.

She talks to the children a little bit, but then some of them are still very young and she's very sensitive to what she thinks they can cope with and she doesn't want to traumatize them, frighten them.
She only wants to give them what she feels they are able to handle.
The big one she tells more.
She's very close to Rachel.
She's-- she's the one that she has always spoken French to.

She looked after her quite regularly when she was tiny, in a way that she never had to with any of the others, although she has them very often and they're very-- they're mutually very fond of each other.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There's a responsibility that go-- that goes with being the daughter of a survivor in that one feels one is replacing a lost generation, or so many that were lost.
And one feels that one wants to carry on.
And the idea of [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is always with us in a lot  of what we do and how we act and how we conduct our lives.
I think that's what I wanted to say.
Yes.
I didn't mention [INAUDIBLE], which I witnessed with several of the kommando What happened was we were-- we were from that day onwards when we-- we were caught at the kommando [INAUDIBLE].
We were from then on only in [? schaff ?] kommandos.
And that time, we were working very near the crematoriums.
I thought there was an eclipse.
It-- it was dark.
It was black.
And yet, it was in the afternoon, because we knew we finished at dark.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suddenly, we saw people being dragged, like all strung up, one after the other.
And they didn't notice us yet, that we were still working, and they said to us that we have to right away get out of here and go back to our bags.

What we heard afterwards, because there was a big appeal later, was that this was a group of people, amongst them was Mala, and some men who had tried to get-- tried to get-- get out of Auschwitz that was very well organized, but were caught the last minute and were brought back.
It was-- they were going to tell the world what was going on in October, '44. '45-- '44.
It was October '44.
And we felt terrible that these people were caught.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing we heard was that there was a big appeal, a call up.
The whole camp was out and these people were going to be executed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also heard that Mala was amongst them, and we understood that she tried-- I can only repeat from what I heard myself in the camp.
I don't know if this was actually a fact-- that she tried to cut her wrists before she was being hung.
And eventually, she wasn't even dead, they told us.
That's what they told us in the camp.
And she was thrown into the-- into the crematorium.
And she had a horrible end.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we actually saw her being brought back at the time.
We happened to work exactly there between the crematorium and a, a, a fence where they were being-- behind them they were being brought back.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I suppose there are different stories about what happened to them, but that's what we saw and what we were told immediately after.
Yes, I-- I'm coming back to Bergen-Belsen now.
We stayed on for another few weeks. 'Till February we stayed on, with the same terrible conditions.
And by then, we were also full of lice because I can't remember the delousing, but I remember that we tried to wash ourselves in ice cold water outside and we tried to keep ourselves clean.
But we were covered with [INAUDIBLE] from no proper food, no proper hygiene.
And we were full of boils.
They were bothering us and the lice were bothering us.
And we were hungry, starving and yet we were beaten all the time.
And I had the impression that they had mostly criminals there.
Kramer was now the chief of-- the kommando of Bergen-Belsen and he gave us the same treatment as Auschwitz.
Well, on the 2nd-- on the 2nd of February or something like that-- end of February-- we were again-- there was a big selection and this time, again, a group was chosen for transport.
And I was amongst the 500 or 700 that were chosen and-- and Janine.
Every time we tried to keep together.
Her sister, now, we didn't know what happened to her sister.
And there was the other girl called Susanne was with us.
When we arrived in Raguhn, which is, I understand near Dresden, we were taken-- we were taken to barracks.
And the guards at the time, when they saw our pitiful state, they said-- well, a little bit kinder with us, and they were a    bit nicer, and I think they gave us a little bit of food.
And we thought that it was paradise.
It didn't last very long because Kramer came as a commandant, and [INAUDIBLE] he came with his guards.
So that was finished.
Then the--
The same-- the same
conditions.
We were sent-- we were going to that factory.
It was-- they manufactured-- they manufactured pieces for airplanes.
I-- Janine and I were given the job-- what would I call it?
Sol-- Soldering?
Soldering?
Or something-- Cutting out, soldering, things like that.
And we were working mostly sometimes at daytime, sometimes nighttime.
The conditions were very bad.
By that time, we had such diarrhea.
And on top of it, no food.
And one fat German who passed us once, he says, tomorrow I bring you-- tomorrow I'll bring you food.
So I said we were already dreaming of getting food.
And the next day when we arrived, we opened that little drawer to look what he brought us.
He had brought us potato peels.
And we knew that, if we eat potato peels, our diarrhea would get worse.
So of course, we-- we didn't eat it.
Anyway, people that were with us, like [INAUDIBLE], who is also a survivor and lives in Paris and is trying to make a film about her story, she was also with us.
And she was only 15 and she was very short.
And she was working on a piece of metal that was so big that you hardly saw her head.
Just her head you saw, and she was trying to turn with other girls that big wheel.
And some were just making tools.
And the Germans saw exactly the way we looked, the way we were-- were starving, the way we were full of lice, and we were sick, and everything.
But I don't remember that one person gave us any food or anything.
But what I would like to tell you is something else.
When we came down from Ber-- from Bergen-Belsen into the station of Raguhn, we had to walk through the town.
And we could smell the smell of fresh bread.
And we were so hungry.
And the water was coming out of our mouth, you know.
We were so hungry.
And we were dirty.
We must have been dirty.
As we walked through the town, the only thing I can remember was somebody spitting at us.
There was no pity.
They were spitting at us.
And I couldn't believe that human beings, you know, seeing young girls in such a state could not have any pity, but just spit on us.
So they took us to that camp.
I thought in the beginning, it was-- we thought it was  a paradise compared to the others.
It didn't only last a few days.
And when I-- before I was chosen for work, I will never forget that woman, that guard.
She said, I managed because I was outside to have colorings.
Because I was working outside-- outdoors.
I seemed to have colors.
And I was always chosen for work.
Because you looked healthy.
Healthier.
Yes, I had more-- Yeah, colors.
And she said, this one.
And then the SS-- the other SS-- says, don't take this one.
She's-- she's like a rosy apple completely rotten inside.
That's what-- I said, you, if I could find you again after the war, do you know what I would do to you?
That's what she said.
But she did send me to work.
So we had a lot of bombardments, you see, at that time.
Because it was near Dresden, we could hear it.
So we were quite a bit in shelters.
That saved us for a few hours-- sometimes an hour or 45 minutes of hard work.
But there we stayed till we-- we knew that the Americans can't be far.
And we are praying that they would liberate us in-- in-- in Raguhn.
But no, they-- they took us again.
The guards were horrible.
The tension was horrible.
By that time already, my teeth were giving-- gums were giving trouble.
The [? anthrax ?] that I had was still very bad.
You know, with all the-- it festered.
I was full of boils-- and [INAUDIBLE].
It's a wonder that it healed up, eventually.
And they took us again in a convoy.
They sent-- they took the whole lot of us, put us again in  a train, 750 girls.
And then we were on the road like every other camp was on the road.
You know, we were in a closed wagon.
Some were in open wagons, the men.
And we saw what was going on.
It was terrible.
And so we were-- we were being driven.
We were, I don't how many of us in-- probably again, 100 in a wagon with one SS with us.
One guard with us in the wagon.
And from time to time, they brought a bucket of cold potatoes.
And here and there, if you were lucky, you got a potato.
By that time, you know, your [INAUDIBLE] had changed.
They were not human anymore.
They were starving.
And if you were lucky, you got a potato.
If you were unlucky, you didn't even get a potato.
We had bombardments on the way.
Therefore, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the guard was more frightened than us.
We couldn't care anymore.
So they used to open the train and-- and-- and take shelter.
So on two occasions, when it was open-- the, the train-- I could see that we were all going to die now.
There was no chance.
So I--
Too many-- People dying on the train.
We had I don't know how-- we had, by the time we arrived in Theresienstadt, we had 125 corpses on our train.
So I tried to escape.
I don't know how I had such illusions.
I hid under the train.
But a guard from the sta-- a railway man, far, far away, could see that somebody was under the train and he reported me to our guard.
Of course, they fetched me and put me back in the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A few days-- we were still on that train for several days, and a few days later, another bombardment.
This time we opened again the train, but this time, I could see we were not going to survive that at all.
I tried-- and we were near a forest.
So, I don't know.
My legs really didn't carry me anymore, but I tried to escape.
And he says, stop, or I'll shoot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stopped, but they could have shot.
They didn't shoot.
And he brought me back.
And Janine, my sister-in-law, you know, she said to me, and you would have left me?
To this day, she hasn't forgiven me for having tried to escape.
But I could see-- I, I realized that I don't know how I would have done it.
To run away, the way I looked, with the clothing I had, how I would have done it?
I couldn't care less.
I was trying to escape.
That-- it shows you that there is-- there was still a sparkle of something left that-- I wanted-- I don't know if I wanted to live or if I wanted to die.
I didn't want to die [INAUDIBLE] that kind of way-- this, anyway.
And they brought me back and put me back in the wagon.

By the time we reached Theresienstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the opened the wagons, the people that were in Theresienstadt-- well, the ones that were left were half Jews or people that somehow didn't get deported.
A few of them, but mostly they were mixed marriages and things like that.
They couldn't believe what they saw.
So they had 100-- I heard afterwards it was 125 dead, out of 500.
And that Mr.
Katz, that I showed you the photo, he took it on to take six girls that were in one room to take care of them.
And he found a doctor.
And he came, and I was very, very ill.
I had pneumonia.
I was sick.
Very ill.
Yes.
Well, when I arrived there, it showed how sick I was.
We had to-- so, he-- he found the doctor, because most of them had tried to run away by now to Czechoslovakia, because they weren't-- not far from Prague, you see
So they-- the Germans were trying to run away.
They could see the war was over.
You know, it was a few days before.
It was the beginning of May-- end of April, beginning of May.
So a lot tried to run away, and a lot of doctors also ran away.
Therefore, the hos-- the camp didn't have many doctors anymore.
But he found a doctor.
We must have had a kind of paracetamol or something.
And they started to treat me.
So the next thing they did-- they wetted the blankets in ice cold water and wrapped  me in that.
And I said, now you're going to kill me for good.
And they gave me some paracetamols and they tried to revive me.
And they did it so-- I don't know, a day or two.
The whole time I stayed there.
And I think that's how he brought me back, you know?
Eventually, when the-- when the-- he said to my sister-in-law, you could hide behind a match.
You know, we were so thin.
Well, was it just chance that it had been you and-- and five others who he-- who he helped at that time?
He was one that helped those five.
You see, other people helped others.
What happened afterwards was that they-- when the Russians liberated the camp and there was typhus, and everybody had a medical, and I'm sure I had-- I had typhus too.
But I didn't want to be separated.
I said to [?
Ranin, ?] if I get separated now, we will-- we won't see each other, you see?
What we didn't know at that stage was that the ones that went into the hospital were very well cared for.
They had put barbed wire around the whole place, you know, because they had seen what was going on.
There was typhus in the camp.
So they separated the hospital and all around there so that nobody could get near the cases that were admitted there.
And the others, they just provided with some flour and then egg-- not egg.
Flour and   a bit of margarine or something they gave them.
And they said-- And potatoes, and they said, make something to eat for yourself.
Because by now, we were in school and we had a stove in that classroom.
And-- and so we could-- we should look after ourselves, because they had to look after the very sick and find out if they can save as many as possible.
When they first came in, the Russians, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were looking out being-- trying to be saved.
It was already the 9th of May, nearly, you know?
And we were waiting for the Americans, hoping we-- we will be liberated by the Americans.
But it was the Russians that came in.
Why did you want it to be the Americans?
I don't know.
We thought probably it would be so much easier.
First of all, they speak-- perhaps because they speak English, and we thought if it was America, they'd probably-- they would give us a lot more than-- than anybody else.
But we knew it was the Russians that were coming.
But it was the Russians that liberated the camp.
They came in with their big tanks and they-- and-- and right away, they could see what was going on.
And-- and they started to try and make put order into it.
Because at first, terrible as some of us looked, some girls were raped in our block of the-- It was a whole house.
And the next day, there was a curfew.
Because we met two-- two very nice officers as we were in there, roaming around the camp afterwards.
And they were Jewish.
And they spoke French.
So they explained to us what was happening in Russia with the Jews.
And they promised us that tomorrow they would bring us a couple of eggs, you know?
But as we never saw them again, so they-- they couldn't get to us.
But I must say, they did a lot for to keep the camp-- give it some order, to see that there should be medication, that the very sick should be hospitalized.

And afterwards, we heard that they gave them beautiful, white bread, beautiful food-- light food-- and they sat with the sick and fed them by little, with spoons, fed, really, to get-- get them back to life.
In that respect, they did help a great deal.
I didn't-- No, I didn't recover till I  went back.
No.
We just were good enough to walk, somehow walk.
You know, we were on our feet again.
And we had-- I could understand, in those days, there were some women who were older than us and so on.
They wanted to take their revenge.
And I wanted to see the school, or the house, where they saw that I-- that we were-- where we had a roof over our heads.
And I wanted to see-- I saw the hospital.
And I only remember that the hospital was very, very far.
But when I went there now, it was very, very near.
But for me to walk from that place to the hospital was a long way.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I-- I saw the graves, you know, of all the nationalities, and the mass graves and all that.
And I didn't go to that memorial service.
I thought it was too much.
But what I didn't know was that it would bring back all the symptoms I had there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I came back, I had, again, the diarrhea.
I was feeling unwell.
I went to see a doctor.
He said, I don't know why you have that.
I can't understand it.
Yes.
It brought it back.
Although I thought it shouldn't because it was a camp where eventually I was liberated.
From there, we went-- we were then taken over, at one point, by the Americans.
We were taken by trucks again from Theresienstadt, where we were-- by Americans, who gave us different clothing that didn't fit at all-- to Kassel.
And there, we were hospitalized for a few days.
And the nurses-- we gave them a bad time, these nurses.
They were German nurses.
And they tried to be as kind as possible to us.
But they could also see what-- in what conditions we were, and they went around crying all the time.
And we had whi-- proper beds with white sheets and all that and we weren't used to it, really.
And we were given food so that we should be ready for the next journey, which was to Pilsen.
Yes.
We-- we-- we told them what was going on in the camps, you know, and-- and what killers they were, you know, what mass murderers they were.
Whatever we could tell them, we told them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, they were-- there were the young generation and they saw for the first time what was-- what happened and the way we looked.
From there, we went on to-- to Pilsen.
And in Pilsen, there-- we were in a kind of camp.
We were very disappointed by the Americans at the time.
At the time, we were.
Most people have been-- had been all right.
We were given bread.
It was exactly the same bread as the camps, which we couldn't eat.
You know, that black bread-- it was-- I don't know what it was.
It was certainly not flour.
We couldn't eat it.
And we could see that there was another camp, a kind of enclosure, where they must have put the Germans in, or Austrian, or whoever they were.
And we saw them going in with white bread, you know, and good food.
And we couldn't understand that, you know?
We were complaining about it.
But we didn't stay long enough.
We were soon then sorted out by nationalities and brought back then to France.
We were brought up-- brought back by plane from Pilson to Leon.
And there we were-- there it was the first time that I saw proper food.
And we couldn't eat it, you know?
The first time that they gave us meat and potatoes and wine and bread.
And we couldn't eat it.
When you say we were brought back, who was--
The French-- French girls.
The French girls were brought back to France and the other nationalities went in other directions.
But the planes left from Pilsen.
From Theresienstadt to Pilsen, from Pilsen to different directions.
I just met somebody who was exactly the same-- took the same route from Theresientstadt-- he was only a child, with his mother, and went from Pilsen to Amsterdam.
[PAUSES FOR 67 SECONDS]
